results.title: UKMTO Update: Volatile Security Environment Across Hormuz, Bab al Mandab and the Arabian Gulf
results.author: Dryad Global
results.text:
UKMTO Update: Volatile Security Environment Across Hormuz, Bab al Mandab and the Arabian Gulf

[Maritime Risk Intelligence Blog](https://channel16.dryadglobal.com/)

# UKMTO Update: Volatile Security Environment Across Hormuz, Bab al Mandab and the Arabian Gulf

Written by [Dryad Global](https://channel16.dryadglobal.com/author/dryad-global)| March 1, 2026 at 8:00 PM

On 01 March 2026, UKMTO issued Update 002 to Advisory 003-26, highlighting a highly volatile maritime security environment in the Arabian Gulf (also known as Persian Gulf), Gulf of Oman, North Arabian Sea, Bab al Mandab (BAM), and the Strait of Hormuz due to significant ongoing regional military activity.

This includes a substantial military presence contributing to elevated threats to commercial shipping, with risks of miscalculation or misidentification near military units, sensitive maritime facilities, and infrastructure. Mariners face potential for VHF hailing, directed communications from military units, and significant GNSS/electronic interference (including disruptions to AIS, positioning, navigation, and communications systems), which may be intermittent and unpredictable.

Claims of closure or transit restr
highlights:
On 01 March 2026, UKMTO issued Update 002 to Advisory 003-26, highlighting a highly volatile maritime security environment in the Arabian Gulf (also known as Persian Gulf), Gulf of Oman, North Arabian Sea, Bab al Mandab (BAM), and the Strait of Hormuz due to significant ongoing regional military activity.
[...]
This includes a substantial military presence contributing to elevated threats to commercial shipping, with risks of miscalculation or misidentification near military units, sensitive maritime facilities, and infrastructure. Mariners face potential for VHF hailing, directed communications from military units, and significant GNSS/electronic interference (including disruptions to AIS, positioning, navigation, and communications systems), which may be intermittent and unpredictable.
[...]
Claims of closure or transit restrictions in the Strait of Hormuz continue to circulate via open source reporting and VHF, but no official closure has been communicated through recognized channels such as NAVAREA warnings or IMO broadcasts; such VHF indications do not constitute a legal suspension of transit passage under UNCLOS. Broader regional instability extends threats beyond the Strait of Hormuz, including elevated tensions in the Bab al Mandab. JMIC guidance urges continuous VHF Channel 16 watch, AIS transmission per policy, adherence to Traffic Separation Schemes and corridors, professional responses to hails, enhanced risk assessments (considering military activity and insurance), and immediate reporting of unusual activity, suspicious approaches, interference, or incidents to UKMTO or other centers. The situation is under close monitoring with further updates expected as warranted.
results.summary:
Summary:
- The UKMTO advisory flags a highly volatile security environment around the Strait of Hormuz as of 1 March 2026, driven by significant regional military activity in the Arabian Gulf, Gulf of Oman, North Arabian Sea, Bab al Mandab, and Hormuz.
- Implications for watchstanders at the Strait of Hormuz:
  - Elevated risk of miscalculation or misidentification near military units and sensitive facilities; require heightened situational awareness.
  - Increased probability of VHF hails and directed communications from military units; maintain Channel 16 watch and exercise professional, disciplined responses to hails.
  - Potential GNSS/electronic interference affecting AIS, positioning, navigation, and communications; plan for degraded navigation and redundancy.
  - Reports of transit restrictions or closures in the Strait circulate unofficially, but there is no official UNCLOS-based closure; do not treat open-source closures as legal suspensions.
  - Emphasis on adhering to Traffic Separation Schemes and established corridors; conduct enhanced risk assessments considering ongoing military activity and insurance considerations.
  - Immediate reporting of unusual or suspicious activity, approaches, interference, or incidents to UKMTO or equivalent centers.
- Practical watchstanding actions:
  - Maintain continuous VHF Channel 16 monitoring; be prepared for military-directed instructions.
  - Verify AIS and GNSS integrity; cross-check with radar and other navigation aids due to potential interference.
  - Reinforce threat awareness for potential rapid changes in security conditions near chokepoints.
  - Ensure robust reporting protocols and bypass/mitigation plans for any loss of navigation data or communications.
- Overall takeaway: For Strait of Hormuz watchstanders, expect heightened, dynamic security risk with possible military activity-induced disruptions; adopt rigorous watch routines, confirm communication channels, and prepare for rapid operational adjustments as updates are issued. Further in-depth guidance is available from UKMTO/Channel 16 analyses.

results.title: Strait of Hormuz Traffic Grinds to Near Halt as Security Threat Remains ‘Critical,’ Advisory Warns
results.author: Mike Schuler
results.text:
Strait of Hormuz Traffic Grinds to Near Halt as Security Threat Remains ‘Critical,’ Advisory Warns

FILE PHOTO: Tankers are seen off the coast of the Fujairah, as Iran vows to close the Strait of Hormuz, amid the U.S.-Israel conflict with Iran, in Fujairah, United Arab Emirates, March 3, 2026. REUTERS/Amr Alfiky/File Photo

# Strait of Hormuz Traffic Grinds to Near Halt as Security Threat Remains ‘Critical,’ Advisory Warns

Commercial shipping through the Strait of Hormuz has slowed to a near standstill as missile attacks, drone threats, and widespread electronic interference continue to [rattle the region](https://gcaptain.com/tag/iran-conflict/), according to the latest maritime security advisory from the Joint Maritime Information Center (JMIC).

In its latest update, JMIC said the regional maritime threat environment remains highly volatile amid the escalating conflict involving the United States, Israel, and Iran, warning that the risk to merchant shipping across the Arabian Gulf, Strait of Hormuz, and northern Arabian Sea remains “CRITICAL,” meaning an attack is considered almost certain.

While the strait technically remains open to navigation, vessel movements have collapse
highlights:
Commercial shipping through the Strait of Hormuz has slowed to a near standstill as missile attacks, drone threats, and widespread electronic interference continue to rattle the region, according to the latest maritime security advisory from the Joint Maritime Information Center (JMIC).
[...]
In its latest update, JMIC said the regional maritime threat environment remains highly volatile amid the escalating conflict involving the United States, Israel, and Iran, warning that the risk to merchant shipping across the Arabian Gulf, Strait of Hormuz, and northern Arabian Sea remains “CRITICAL,” meaning an attack is considered almost certain.
[...]
While the strait technically remains open to navigation, vessel movements have collapsed as shipowners and charterers reassess the risks of transiting one of the world’s most important maritime chokepoints.A review of vessel tracking data cited in the advisory shows that traffic through the waterway has nearly stopped.
[...]
“Recent AIS review indicates transits have reduced to single-digit levels, with only one confirmed commercial transit observed in the past 24 hours,” the advisory said.
[...]
Data from MarineTraffic.com shows tanker traffic through Strait of Hormuz down by more than 90%. “Unlike several other vessel segments where movements have largely ceased, some tankers are still traveling east and west through the strait, with a number of voyages occurring under AIS blackouts,” says Matt Wright, Principal Freight Analyst at Kpler, parent company to MarineTraffic.com.
[...]
A containership transiting the Strait
[...]
region. The
[...]
is one of
[...]
across the region in
[...]
The JMIC advisory also highlighted widespread GPS and GNSS interference affecting vessels operating in the region, including AIS anomalies, positional inaccuracies, and degraded communications systems. Shipmasters were urged to closely monitor navigation equipment and be prepared to rely on radar, visual bearings, and traditional navigation techniques if satellite-based systems become unreliable.
[...]
that the maritime security environment could
[...]
missile activity expands closer to major shipping routes or if strikes occur against ports, offshore infrastructure
[...]
or terminals supporting Gulf energy exports
[...]
Shipping companies operating in the region have been advised to conduct enhanced voyage risk assessments before entering the Gulf, maintain reporting links with the United Kingdom Maritime Trade Operations (UKMTO) center and the Maritime Security Centre – Indian Ocean (MSCIO), and follow established Best Management Practices for maritime security.
results.summary:
Summary:
- The Strait of Hormuz remains a highly volatile maritime security hotspot amid US-Israel-Iran hostilities, with the Joint Maritime Information Center (JMIC) describing the risk to merchant shipping as “CRITICAL”—almost certain to see attacks.
- Traffic is effectively paused: AIS data shows transits in single digits, with roughly a 90% drop in tanker movements; some vessels continue under AIS blackouts, but no formal closure has been declared.
- The region’s chokepoint status for global energy markets persists (roughly 20% of world oil and significant LNG exports), amplifying the impact of any disruption on security and logistics.
- Active threats include missile and drone attacks, projectile strikes on vessels, and widespread electronic interference (GPS/GNSS problems, AIS anomalies) that degrade navigation.
- Operational guidance for watchstanders: anticipate degraded satellite navigation, rely on radar, visual bearings, and traditional navigation methods, and monitor all navigation sensors closely for GNSS degradation or loss.
- Overall relevance for watchstanding: heightened threat environment requires rigorous security watch, contingency routing, heightened AIS/GNSS integrity checks, and readiness to revert to non-satellite navigation techniques to maintain safe operations in the strait.

results.title: Iran Strait of Hormuz Risk Advisory March 2026 - Maritime News
results.text:
Iran Strait of Hormuz Risk Advisory March 2026 - Maritime News

# Iran's Maritime Risk Increases; Strait of Hormuz Traffic Near Halt Amid Advisories

US MARAD, UKMTO and JMIC maritime advisories warn of high risks in the Persian Gulf, Strait of Hormuz and Gulf of Oman following over 20 confirmed incidents involving commercial vessels since late February. Transit volumes remain severely diminished while operators should coordinate with NCAGS while keeping a distance from naval units.

Maritime operators face heightened risks in the Persian Gulf, Strait of Hormuz, and Gulf of Oman as advisories from major authorities underscore ongoing disruptions to commercial shipping. Between March 8 and March 20, 2026, reports confirmed more than 20 incidents involving vessels and offshore facilities, contributing to near-paralyzed transit through key chokepoints.

## US MARAD Issues Advisory 2026-004 on March 16

The US Maritime Administration released Advisory 2026-004 on March 16, reinforcing high risk of attacks against commercial shipping in the Persian Gulf, Strait of Hormuz, and Gulf of Oman. Masters are instructed to maintain at least 30 nautical miles from US naval units, cooperate with
highlights:
US MARAD, UKMTO and JMIC maritime advisories warn of high risks in the Persian Gulf, Strait of Hormuz and Gulf of Oman following over 20 confirmed incidents involving commercial vessels since late February. Transit volumes remain severely diminished while operators should coordinate with NCAGS while keeping a distance from naval units.
[...]
Maritime operators face heightened risks in the Persian Gulf, Strait of Hormuz, and Gulf of Oman as advisories from major authorities underscore ongoing disruptions to commercial shipping. Between March 8 and March 20, 2026, reports confirmed more than 20 incidents involving vessels and offshore facilities, contributing to near-paralyzed transit through key chokepoints.
[...]
The US Maritime Administration released Advisory 2026-004 on March 16, reinforcing high risk of attacks against commercial shipping in the Persian Gulf, Strait of Hormuz, and Gulf of Oman. Masters are instructed to maintain at least 30 nautical miles from US naval units, cooperate with coalition queries, and coordinate transits with NAVCENT NCAGS. The advisory warns against complying with over-the-horizon VHF or email instructions from Iranian forces, advising vessels to log encounters and continue on track.
[...]
The Joint Maritime Information Center (JMIC), via UKMTO, elevated the maritime threat level to critical across the Persian Gulf, Strait of Hormuz, and Gulf of Oman. More than 20 incidents have occurred since February 28 escalations, including attacks on vessels, navigation system interference, and risks to port infrastructure. The risk of further disruptions remains high.
[...]
UKMTO has reported over 20 maritime incidents in and around the Strait since March 1. Operators are urged to monitor VHF 16, report abnormal activity, and stay aware of electronic interference and misidentification risks.
[...]
Commercial traffic through the Strait of Hormuz remains extremely limited. On March 15, only three outbound crossings were recorded, with no inbound transits, marking continued suppression far below normal levels. Maritime activity across the Gulf stayed heavily disrupted, distorting cargo flows and energy shipments.
[...]
Since the conflict's onset, 20 confirmed incidents targeted commercial vessels and offshore infrastructure, primarily cargo ships: 40 percent bulk carriers, 30 percent tankers, 20 percent container vessels, and 10 percent service vessels. About 45 percent of affected vessels had recent UAE port calls, with others from Iraq, Thailand, Vietnam, and Brazil.
[...]
, MARAD, and flag directives. Dual-theater risks combine Hormuz with Red Sea exposures, prompting route redist
results.summary:
Summary:
- The Strait of Hormuz and adjacent Persian Gulf area are at heightened security risk through March 2026, due to a surge of hostile incidents, navigation disruptions, and broader geopolitical pressures.
- Key authorities (US MARAD, UKMTO, JMIC) have issued repeated advisories warning of high risk to commercial shipping, with threat levels raised to critical. Over 20 incidents involving vessels or offshore facilities have been reported since late February, severely reducing transit volumes.
- Operational guidance for watchstanders:
  - Maintain distance: keep at least 30 nautical miles from US naval units per MARAD advisory; coordinate with NAVCENT NCAGS for transits.
  - Do not follow over-the-horizon or unofficial instructions from Iranian forces; log encounters and stay on course.
  - Monitor VHF channel 16; report abnormal activity; be alert for electronic interference, misidentification, and GNSS spoofing/jamming.
  - Expect and prepare for challenging navigation: mine threats, missile/UAV/USV risks, debris, and restricted port access.
- Traffic patterns and risks:
  - Transit volumes through the Strait of Hormuz have plummeted; March 15 saw only three outbound crossings and no inbound transits.
  - Vessel mix affected includes bulk carriers (40%), tankers (30%), containers (20%), and service vessels (10%). A significant share of affected ships had recent UAE port calls.
- Port and area-wide measures:
  - Ports such as Ras Al Khaimah (RAK) restrict Iranian-registered vessels/cargo.
  - Fujairah reports intermittent GPS spoofing and nearby fires.
  - Norway raised MARSEC/ISPS Level 3 for Norwegian-flagged ships in the region, signaling enhanced protective measures.
- Implications for watchstanders:
  - Expect irregular port calls, increased caution during approach/leaving energy hubs, and potential need for anchor readiness (3 nautical miles off major ports).
  - Maintain situational awareness of ongoing regional tensions, navigational hazards, and disruptive advisories from MARAD, UKMTO, and JMIC.

results.title: Strait of Hormuz GPS Spoofing & AIS Manipulation
results.author: Dimitra Sotirchos
results.text:
Strait of Hormuz GPS Spoofing & AIS Manipulation 

By Saleem Khan, Chief Data & Analytics Officer – Pole Star Global

Since 5 March, Pole Star Global has tracked 3,396 vessels in and around the Strait of Hormuz. Roughly 1 in 15 of them – 231 ships – are exhibiting suspicious positional anomalies. “Jumping” implausible distances, going briefly “ghosted,” or reappearing in patterns consistent with GPS and AIS interference. This is no longer a story about isolated glitches; it’s a systematic, geographically clustered distortion of reality at sea.

At the same time, we are seeing a near-total cessation of commercial traffic through the Strait, and some ships leaving the region are broadcasting extraordinary “destinations” such as “China Owner and All Crew”, turning what should be a navigational field into a live political message: don’t shoot, we’re not your target.

Beyond the chokepoint, about 540 oil tankers carrying an estimated 314 million barrels are currently at sea with no fixed destination, listed simply as “awaiting orders” – effectively a $30 billion floating oil exchange waiting for a safe route and a safe buyer.

Pole Star Global fuses AIS, satellite detections, ownership,
highlights:
Strait of Hormuz GPS Spoofing & AIS Manipulation 

By Saleem Khan, Chief Data & Analytics Officer – Pole Star Global
[...]
Since 5 March, Pole Star Global has tracked 3,396 vessels in and around the Strait of Hormuz. Roughly 1 in 15 of them – 231 ships – are exhibiting suspicious positional anomalies. “Jumping” implausible distances, going briefly “ghosted,” or reappearing in patterns consistent with GPS and AIS interference. This is no longer a story about isolated glitches; it’s a systematic, geographically clustered distortion of reality at sea.
[...]
At the same time, we are seeing a near-total cessation of commercial traffic through the Strait, and some ships leaving the region are broadcasting extraordinary “destinations” such as “China Owner and All Crew”, turning what should be a navigational field into a live political message: don’t shoot, we’re not your target.
[...]
Beyond the chokepoint, about 540 oil tankers carrying an estimated 314 million barrels are currently at sea with no fixed destination, listed simply as “awaiting orders” – effectively a $30 billion floating oil exchange waiting for a safe route and a safe buyer.
[...]
Pole Star Global
[...]
analytics to help governments
[...]
from manipulation. Our data offers an unusually
[...]
how conflict,
[...]
, and digital
[...]
are reshaping global trade in
[...]
The image below shows intentional AIS manipulation by a vessel called the NV Aquamarine (in red trails). This vessel was travelling at an astounding 102 knots (188 km/hr) from the Strait to the Iran/Iraq border. This is impossible for a tanker that has a capacity to carry 350,000 barrels of oil worth $35M at current spot rates.
results.summary:
Summary:
- The Strait of Hormuz is experiencing systemic GPS spoofing and AIS (Automatic Identification System) manipulation, with about 1 in 15 vessels showing suspicious positional anomalies (roughly 231 of 3,396 tracked ships).
- anomalies include implausible jumps, brief “ghosting,” and reappearances in patterns indicating deliberate interference rather than random glitches.
- Commercial traffic through the Strait has largely halted, while vessels leaving the region display provocative destinations (e.g., “China Owner and All Crew”) as political signaling.
- A large pool of vessels—about 540 oil tankers with ~314 million barrels—are at sea with no fixed destination, effectively forming a floating oil exchange worth roughly $30 billion.
- Pole Star Global combines AIS, satellite detections, ownership data, and behavioral analytics to help governments, insurers, and shipping companies distinguish malfunctions from manipulation and to anticipate where disruptions may occur next.
- An illustrative example shows AIS manipulation by the vessel NV Aquamarine, which appeared to travel at 102 knots (188 km/h)—an impossible speed for a 350,000-barrel tanker—highlighting how spoofing can obscure true risk and route safety.
- Overall relevance to Strait of Hormuz watchstanding: persistent electronic deception is compounding security risks, potentially affecting navigational certainty, risk assessment, fuel supply chains, and decision-making for asset protection, convoy staging, and contingency routing in a high-tension choke point.

# Dark ships and shadow fleets – what is crossing 'closed' Strait of Hormuz?

Iran's move to shut the Strait of Hormuz has brought shipping through the 24-mile stretch of water close to a standstill.

Only a handful of vessels have transited in recent days, many switching off their tracking systems or linked to "shadow fleets".

"They can't physically close a waterway of that size, but the threat is there", said Richard Meade, editor-in-chief of maritime risk company Lloyd's List Intelligence.

Mr Meade said Iran has a proven history of using everything from ballistic missiles to unmanned aerial and seaborne drones.

What is making it through?

Sky News' Data & Forensics team has focused on 13 ships that have transited the Strait of Hormuz between 2 and 9 March.

We know the real number of crossings is much higher, as some ships turn their tracking system off, a practice known as going "dark".

Data from marine analytics firm IMF Portwatch suggests that in normal times,
highlights:
Iran's move to shut the Strait of Hormuz has brought shipping through the 24-mile stretch of water close to a standstill.
[...]
Only a handful of vessels have transited in recent days, many switching off their tracking systems or linked to "shadow fleets".
[...]
We know the real number of crossings is much higher, as some ships turn their tracking system off, a practice known as going "dark".
[...]
Of the ships we have identified passing through the Strait of Hormuz, many have links to Iran, China or Russia. There are also ships from Greece, India, the UAE and Singapore.
[...]
Shadow fleet tankers dominate current crossings of the Strait of Hormuz, according to Lloyd's List Intelligence.
[...]
They found that, of the 13 large oil and gas carriers that crossed between 2-9 March, eight were classified as part of the so-called shadow fleet.
[...]
Thirteen vessels in or near the Strait of Hormuz have been attacked since Iran blocked the waterway, according to the International Maritime Organisation (IMO).
[...]
According to United Kingdom Maritime Trade Operations (UKMTO), a total of 17 incidents from 28 February to 11 March have affected vessels operating in and around the Arabian Gulf, Strait of Hormuz and Gulf of Oman.
[...]
A few hours later, in the Straight of Hormuz close to Oman, a security officer reported their cargo ship was hit by an unknown projectile which resulted in a fire onboard. The vessel requested assistance and the crew evacuated.
[...]
The ship was 30 nautical miles southeast of the Kuwait coast. UKMTO reported an oil spill because of the incident.
[...]
There has been a huge surge in GPS jamming in the region. Hundreds of ships jump around the map and then cluster in very small areas.
[...]
GPS ship jamming is
[...]

[...]
Tracking data from MarineTraffic shows container ship 'Maersk Cincinnati' rerouting its course away from the strait.
[...]
On 2 March, the data shows the ship did an almost U-turn at around 2pm UTC. It continued to retreat from the area until 4 March, finally returning to the Port of Salalah in Oman, heading to the Gulf of Kutch on 10 March, where it appeared "laded", heavily loaded.
[...]
Mr Meade said: "We're seeing a number of ships doing U-turns. They're likely getting orders to go and perform alternative operations. Now, that's fine for the ships that are headed into the Gulf, but for the ships that are already there, they're essentially stuck."
results.summary:
Summary:
- Iran’s blockade of the Strait of Hormuz has sharply reduced traffic, with many vessels “going dark” by turning off tracking or joining so-called shadow fleets.
- Shadow fleet vessels, carrying sanctioned oil or linked to Iran, Russia, or Venezuela, dominate recent crossings (8 of 13 major tankers between March 2–9 were shadow fleet).
- The limited crossings include ships from Greece, India, UAE, Singapore, and others; several flagged by diverse registries (US, Marshall Islands, Gibraltar, Bahamas, Panama, India).
- Attacks and incidents near Hormuz have surged: at least 17 UKMTO-reported incidents Feb 28–Mar 11, including four vessel attacks on Mar 1 and multiple subsequent strikes, some causing fires or hull damage; crews evacuated in several cases.
- Analysts note Iran has a history of using missiles, drones (ballistic, aerial, sea-based) to pressure traffic, though they can’t physically seal the strait entirely.
- The situation creates significant maritime security risks: increased security advisories, potential escalation, and disruption to global oil/gas shipments via a chokepoint that normally handles about 30,000 ships/year (≈82 per day).
- For watchstanding: expect ongoing monitoring of:
  - Vessel movements with “dark” AIS behavior and shadow-fleet activity
  - Attacks, near-miss events, and security advisories issued by UKMTO and IMO
  - Flag states and ownership profiles of transiting ships
  - Changes in transit patterns, route diversions, and potential escalation risk around Hormuz
- Practical watchstanding implications: heightened alert for suspicious activity, coordination with naval/MOST partners, and readiness to reroute or provide assistance to vessels under threat.

results.title: Hormuz Active Crisis 5 Ships Hit Traffic Collapses | gCaptain
results.author: Mike Schuler
results.text:
Hormuz Active Crisis 5 Ships Hit Traffic Collapses | gCaptain

Oil tankers pass through the Strait of Hormuz, December 21, 2018. REUTERS/Hamad I Mohammed

# Strait of Hormuz Enters Active Crisis: Five Ships Hit, Traffic Collapses, Tanker Markets Brace for Historic Shock

The Strait of Hormuz has entered its most dangerous phase in years after a concentrated wave of missile and drone attacks struck five commercial vessels across the Gulf of Oman and UAE waters. With ports temporarily suspending operations, insurers reassessing war-risk exposure, and AIS data showing transits collapsing, the region’s maritime security environment has shifted from elevated tension to active crisis.

As of Sunday, the Joint Maritime Information Center (JMIC) has [elevated](https://www.ukmto.org/-/media/ukmto/products/update-002---001---jmic-advisory-note-28_feb_2026_final.pdf?rev=54f3ebd757e14bccb7e6ac9b62108bcb) the regional maritime threat level to CRITICAL after confirmed missile and drone attacks against multiple commercial vessels in the Gulf of Oman, the Musandam approaches, and UAE coastal waters — a designation indicating an attack is considered almost certain.

In just 24 hours, five vessels w

The Strait of Hormuz has entered its most dangerous phase in years after a concentrated wave of missile and drone attacks struck five commercial vessels across the Gulf of Oman and UAE waters. With ports temporarily suspending operations, insurers reassessing war-risk exposure, and AIS data showing transits collapsing, the region’s maritime security environment has shifted from elevated tension to active crisis.
[...]
As of Sunday, the Joint Maritime Information Center (JMIC) has elevated the regional maritime threat level to CRITICAL after confirmed missile and drone attacks against multiple commercial vessels in the Gulf of Oman, the Musandam approaches, and UAE coastal waters — a designation indicating an attack is considered almost certain.

[...]
Stand-off missile and

[...]
- Jebel Ali briefly suspended operations following an aerial interception that caused a fire before resuming limited activity.
- Duqm Port in Oman remains operational after two UAV strikes injured one worker.
- Ras Tanura Refinery in Saudi Arabia was reportedly shut following a drone strike and fire.
- Bahrain temporarily suspended port operations and pilotage services.
- Qatar’s Ras Laffan and Mesaieed ports reported significant GPS signal degradation.
[...]
Significant GNSS/GPS interference is being reported across the Strait’s approaches, compounding navigational risks amid heavy congestion and heightened aerial threats.
[...]
AIS tracking indicates transits through the Strait may have fallen to just 28 vessels over a 24-hour period — roughly an 80% decline from the historical daily average near 138 vessels.
[...]
Cargo transits dropped from 98 combined eastbound/westbound movements on February 28 to just 18 on March 1. Tanker movements fell even more sharply, from 50 combined transits to just three.
[...]
JMIC cautioned the drop may reflect a short-term pause rather than a structural halt, but insurers and operators are clearly reassessing risk.
[...]
“Insurance availability may now act as a primary gating factor for transit decisions independent of formal navigational closure,” JMIC noted.
[...]
Even absent an official blockade, the combination of aerial threat, port suspensions, insurance withdrawals, and GPS disruption is effectively constraining commercialadfad movement.
results.summary:
Summary:
- The Strait of Hormuz has shifted from elevated tension to an active security crisis, with a concentrated wave of missile and drone attacks striking five vessels in the Gulf of Oman and nearby waters.
- Maritime threat level has been raised to CRITICAL by JMIC, signaling attacks are considered almost certain and all merchant vessels are at risk regardless of flag or nationality.
- Recent incidents involved:
  - MKD VYOM: crude tanker struck, explosion and engine-room fire; one crew death; under control and tow slated.
  - SKYLIGHT: fire after attack; four crew injured; entire crew evacuated.
  - STENA IMPERATIVE (US-flagged): hit while berthed in Bahrain; one shipyard worker killed, two seriously injured.
  - HERCULES STAR: fire but no injuries; vessel resumed operation.
  - OCEAN ELECTRA: near-miss west of Sharjah; no damage.
- Broader security implications:
  - All vessels in the region face heightened risk; no single operational/political linkage identified among attacks.
  - Ongoing stand-off missile and drone threats persist across Hormuz and Gulf of Oman.
  - No confirmed sea mine activity yet; GNSS/GPS interference reported across approaches, aggravating navigation amid congestion and aerial threats.
- Port/energy infrastructure targets and near-term impact:
  - Jebel Ali: brief suspension, then restart of limited activities.
  - Duqm: operational after UAV strikes injured one worker.
  - Ras Tanura: reportedly shut due to drone strike and fire.
  - Bahrain: port operations/pilotage temporarily suspended.
  - Ras Laffan and Mesaieed (Qatar): significant GPS signal degradation observed.
- Practical watchstanding relevance:
  - Heightened vigilance for vessel transits, especially commercial tankers and bulkers through Hormuz and Strait approaches.
  - Increased need for robust AIS/GNSS integrity checks, enhanced traffic management, and collision avoidance due to congestion and GNSS interference.
  - Expect potential port/anchorages disruptions and elevated insurance/war-risk considerations for vessels transiting the area.
  - Maintain current threat advisories and coordinate with regional QHSSE/joint maritime information centers for real-time security updates.

# Container ships stranded near Strait of Hormuz as AIS disruptions surge

- [Editor](https://globalmaritimehub.com/author/david)
- March 12, 2026

Container ships around the Strait of Hormuz are experiencing unprecedented AIS signal disruptions, according to industry analysts Alphaliner.

Alphaliner live satellite vessel tracking shows that 48 container ships in the waters west of the Strait of Hormuz currently have their transponders switched off or are broadcasting spoofed positions, as of 10 March.

Disruption in the area reached even higher levels earlier in the month. A record 65 container ships registered lost or manipulated signals on 4 March, when the number of vessels with visible and disrupted transmissions was split exactly in half.

Abnormally high levels of signal disruption in the region persisted over the following days, with 62 ships recorded on 6 March, 63 on 7 March, and 60 on 8 March.

For comparison, during the June 2025 Twelve-Day War, the

Disruption in the area reached even higher levels earlier in the month. A record 65 container ships registered lost or manipulated signals on 4 March, when the number of vessels with visible and disrupted transmissions was split exactly in half.
[...]
Abnormally high levels of signal disruption in the region persisted over the following days, with 62 ships recorded on 6 March, 63 on 7 March, and 60 on 8 March.
[...]
For comparison, during the June 2025 Twelve-Day War, the number of container ships around Hormuz operating with disrupted signals peaked at 32 vessels.
[...]
The last container ship recorded leaving the Persian (Arabian) Gulf via the Strait of Hormuz was observed on 5 March, and was a sub-3,000 TEU vessel. Since then, no additional container ships have exited the Gulf.
[...]
In total, 131 container ships remain stranded west of the Strait of Hormuz, according to live vessel monitoring.
[...]
### Strait of Horm
[...]
cargo bookings resume for Gulf states
[...]
### Strait of Hormuz cargo bookings resume for Gulf states
results.summary:
Summary:
- The article reports widespread AIS (automatic identification system) disruptions for container ships west of the Strait of Hormuz, with vessels either turning off transponders or broadcasting spoofed positions.
- Peak disruption reached 65 ships on 4 March, then settled around 60–63 ships in the following days; as of 10 March, 48 ships were affected. Overall, 131 container ships remained stranded west of Hormuz due to the disruptions.
- The disruptions are being tracked by Alphaliner, indicating significant impacts on vessel visibility, movement, and potentially security vulnerabilities in a high-risk chokepoint region.
- Contextual comparison: disruption levels during the 2025 Twelve-Day War peaked at 32 ships, suggesting a notable intensification in early March 2026.
- Operational impact: No further ships have exited the Gulf since 5 March, suggesting a possible escalation of restrictions or defensive posture by operators and navies in the area.
- The article situates these disruptions within broader Gulf security and shipping risk concerns, including potential threats to navigation, flow of global trade through the Strait of Hormuz, and related bunker/fueling risks.

Relevance to Strait of Hormuz watchstanding:
- AIS disruption patterns directly affect maritime situational awareness, complicating threat detection, convoy management, and collision avoidance in one of the world’s most critical strategic chokepoints.
- The concentration of stranded vessels signals elevated operational risk for navigation, port calls, and rapid response to incidents, which watchstanders must monitor for potential escalations or rescue/repositioning needs.
- The persistence and volume of disruptions necessitate heightened vigilance, cross-checking with alternative tracking feeds, and coordination with allied naval forces and regional authorities to maintain safe passage and deter malign activity.
- Analysts should consider correlating these AIS anomalies with reported security events, shipping insurance implications, and potential exploitation by state or non-state actors seeking to disrupt Gulf maritime trade.

If you want, I can tailor this to a watchstanding brief with suggested indicators to monitor (e.g., AIS status trends, hourly vessel counts near Hormuz, crossing/exit patterns) and a one-page action checklist.

results.title: Iran offers proposal allowing ships to exit Oman side of Hormuz free of attack, source says - BusinessWorld Online
results.author: AgarWalEkwensi
results.text:
Iran offers proposal allowing ships to exit Oman side of Hormuz free of attack, source says - BusinessWorld Online

BW Anniversary Reports

Search

[Home](https://www.bworldonline.com/) [World](https://www.bworldonline.com/world/) Iran offers proposal allowing ships to exit Oman side of Hormuz free of...

A 3D-printed oil pump jack and a map showing the Strait of Hormuz and Iran appear in this illustration taken March 2, 2026. — REUTERS

DUBAI — Iran could consider allowing ships to sail freely through the Omani side of the Strait of Hormuz without risk of attack as part of proposals it has offered in negotiations with the United States, providing a deal is clinched to prevent renewed conflict, a source briefed by Tehran said.

The US-Israeli war with Iran has resulted in the largest-ever disruption of global oil and gas supplies due to Iran’s interruption of traffic through the strait, which handles about 20% of the world’s oil and liquefied natural gas flows.

Hundreds of tankers and other ships and 20,000 seafarers have been stuck inside the Gulf since the war began on February 28. A two-week ceasefire came into effect on April 8 and US President Donald Trump said on Wednesday t
highlights:
DUBAI — Iran could consider allowing ships to sail freely through the Omani side of the Strait of Hormuz without risk of attack as part of proposals it has offered in negotiations with the United States, providing a deal is clinched to prevent renewed conflict, a source briefed by Tehran said.
[...]
The US-Israeli war with Iran has resulted in the largest-ever disruption of global oil and gas supplies due to Iran’s interruption of traffic through the strait, which handles about 20% of the world’s oil and liquefied natural gas flows.
[...]
Hundreds of tankers and other ships and 20,000 seafarers have been stuck inside the Gulf since the war began on February 28. A two-week ceasefire came into effect on April 8 and US President Donald Trump said on Wednesday the war was close to over, but control over the Strait of Hormuz remains a key issue in negotiations.
[...]
The source, who declined to be identified due to the sensitivity of the matter, said Iran could be willing to let ships use the other side of the narrow strait in Omani waters without any hindrance from Tehran.
[...]
A Western security source said the proposal to let ships pass through Omani waters unhindered had been in the works although it was not clear if there had been any response from Washington yet.
[...]
The strait, a strip of water only 34 km (21 miles) wide between Iran and Oman, provides passage from the Gulf to the Indian Ocean and is a main route for energy supplies from the Middle East and other vital goods including fertilizers.
[...]
The proposal would
[...]
ideas floated in recent weeks
[...]
which included charging ships for passage through the international waterway and imposing
[...]
on the strait – seen by the global shipping industry as unprecedented unilateral
[...]
Member countries of the UN’s International Maritime Organization agency meeting in London this week pushed back on the idea of a toll being imposed by Iran for ships using the strait, which the IMO has said would “set a dangerous precedent
[...]
Iran’s proposal would also be the first move towards restoring the status quo on sailing through the strait, which had been in place for decades despite periodic seizures by Iran of ships crossing the waterway.
[...]
A so-called two-way traffic separation scheme, which was adopted by the UN’s shipping agency in 1968 with agreement of countries in the region, created the current ship routing system that split sailing corridors through Iranian and Omani waters.
[...]
The US imposed a blockade on oil ships leaving Iranian ports on Monday and broader shipping traffic has remained muted since February 28. — Reuters
results.summary:
Summary:
- Iran reportedly offered a potential concession to ease maritime risk in the Strait of Hormuz by allowing ships to pass freely through the Omani side of the strait, reducing the risk of attack if a broader US-Iran deal is reached.
- The proposal aims to restore the near-prewar status quo and could complement other negotiations to reduce disruption to global energy flows, which have been severely impacted since February.
- Key security implication: if accepted, navigation through Omani waters could become less perilous, potentially lowering the chance of strikes on shipping and easing the two-way traffic routing arrangements; however, uncertainty remains over mine clearance, whether Israeli-linked vessels would be exempt, and the exact conditions tied to any deal with Washington.
- Context: The Strait (34 km wide) is a critical choke point for roughly 20% of global oil and LNG flows, with ongoing shipping disruptions and past Iranian seizures affecting maritime security and insurance costs.
- The IMO and UN-imposed routing schemes (two-way/traffic separation) underpin current safe passage; Iran’s latest proposal seeks to avoid unilateral tolls or sovereignty claims that would destabilize customary maritime conventions.

results.title: The Strait of Hormuz is a data problem, not just a military one - Iran
results.text:
The Strait of Hormuz is a data problem, not just a military one - Iran

# The Strait of Hormuz is a data problem, not just a military one

Since the first tanker pushed through it, the Strait of Hormuz has been treated as a static math problem. You tallied the hulls, weighed the warheads and assumed you knew the score. If you could map the Fifth Fleet’s tonnage against the IRGC’s mine density, you had a working theory on who held the leverage and what a barrel of crude ought to cost. For decades, we looked at those 21 miles of water and saw a cage made of steel.

That logic is now an artifact. The “grey hull” era of deterrence didn’t end with a kinetic explosion. It just quietly stopped being the thing that mattered. What’s happening in the Gulf isn’t a traditional naval confrontation. It’s the violent, accelerating breakdown of a global system that destroyers aren’t equipped to target.

[A note on sourcing: Several of the data points below come from Windward, whose CEO co-authored this piece, and from the maritime data sector in which co-author Erik Bethel’s firm, Mare Liberum, is an active investor. We have flagged these instances and stand behind the underlying figures, which ar
highlights:
Run the numbers. When U.S. and Israeli strikes on Iran kicked off on February 28, traffic through the world’s most critical oil artery didn’t just slow — it cratered by 97% in a single week, according to Windward’s Q1 2026 shipping risk report. Upwards of 800 ships were left idling west of the chokepoint, effectively paralyzed. Of the 142.5 million barrels loaded in March, a staggering 128 million never cleared the gap. By late April, with the ceasefire fraying and tankers still taking hits mid-transit, the Strait is, in the authors’ assessment, closed to commercial traffic.
[...]
The missiles and drones make for good headlines, but they’re a distraction. The real story is that the Strait has gone dark. Not in some poetic sense, but literally. The Automatic Identification System (AIS) — the network that’s supposed to be the “gold standard” for commercial tracking — has stopped telling the truth.
[...]
Ships are vanishing into digital black holes only to materialize hours later on the other side of Hormuz with the transit completed in total silence. On April 21, Windward’s platform identified 296 vessels off Bandar Abbas. Of these, only 74 were transmitting AIS signals — a cooperative rate of roughly 25%.
[...]
Others are caught in the crossfire of GPS spoofing attacks — fake satellite signals, broadcast from shore, that fool a ship’s navigation into thinking it’s somewhere it isn’t. The result is a fleet of tankers whose screens show them circling inland airports or drifting across the Iranian desert. Windward identified at least 30 jamming clusters across Saudi Arabia, Kuwait, the UAE, Qatar, Oman and Iran. Some have gone further, broadcasting the identity numbers of hulls that were scrapped years ago. These are zombie ships — very real tankers operating under the digital signatures of vessels that no longer exist.
[...]
Data suggest AIS is now underreporting Hormuz traffic by half. In Q1 alone, nearly a million GPS jamming incidents hit over 1,100 vessels. Satellite imagery recently caught seven VLCCs — 14 million barrels of capacity — off Iran’s coast with zero digital footprint. Iran claims 11 million barrels exported during a blockade where commercial feeds show a graveyard. Both are true. That is the problem.
results.summary:
Summary:
- The Strait of Hormuz is now a data-centric security problem as much as a naval one. Traditional deterrence—measured by tonnage, warhead counts, and ship counts—fails to capture the current risk environment.
- The key dynamics are about information integrity and cyber/geomonitoring disruption, not just kinetic strikes.
- AIS (automatic identification system) data, used globally to track shipping, is being weaponized: self-reported, unverifiable, and susceptible to spoofing or spoofed signals. This erodes confidence in maritime traffic data, complicating risk assessments for watchstanders.
- Attacks include GPS spoofing, radio jamming, and deployment of “zombie ships” whose AIS signatures misrepresent their actual status or identity. Such deceptions create blind spots for insurers, regulators, ports, and trading desks.
- Real-world impact for watchstanders: sharp reductions in reliable traffic visibility (e.g., 25% AIS cooperation observed by Windward in late April), dramatic declines in transits, and large-scale vessel idling or misrouting. This increases the probability of miscalculation, delays, and mispriced risk.
- The “darkening” of the Strait undermines standard security and risk models that assume verifiable, transparent ship movements; responders must account for data degradation, false signals, and the potential for rapid, unanticipated shifts in traffic flow.
- Practical watchstanding implications: heightened need for corroborating intelligence sources beyond AIS (satellite imagery, open-source intelligence), enhanced cyber-defense for navigational systems, readiness for false-flag or spoofed vessel data, and contingency planning for sudden chokepoint congestion or unexpected ship behavior.

Bottom line for maritime security watchstanders: maintain skepticism toward AIS-based tracking, bolster multi-source verification, anticipate data manipulation tactics (GPS/jamming, spoofing, zombie signatures), and prepare for rapid, opaque changes in traffic that could affect risk pricing, routing decisions, and port-of-call planning.

results.title: Which Asian countries' ships are passing through the Strait of Hormuz? - BBC News
results.text:
Which Asian countries' ships are passing through the Strait of Hormuz? - BBC News

Getty Images

An Indian tanker that passed through the Strait of Hormuz

Osmond Chia

Business reporter

7 April 2026, 00:34 BST

On Monday, US President Donald Trump threatened to take out Iran "in one night" if it failed to agree a deal by 20:00 Washington DC time on Tuesday (00:00GMT Wednesday) to reopen the Strait of Hormuz.

But even before his latest threat some countries had secured deals with Tehran for their vessels to use the key shipping route.

Asian nations - most recently the Philippines - have been particularly keen to reach agreements as [their economies are heavily reliant on energy from the Gulf](http://news.bbc.co.uk/news/articles/c1450zj6n48o).

The critical route has become a global flashpoint after Tehran retaliated to US and Israeli airstrikes by threatening to attack ships in the strait.

Oil prices have [surged since the disruption](http://news.bbc.co.uk/news/articles/c20zgjzz0e4o) to shipping in the narrow waterway, through which a fifth of the world's energy shipments usually transits.

Last week, Trump said [the US did not need the Gulf's oil](http://news.bbc.co.uk/news/vi
highlights:
agree a deal by
[...]
to reopen the Strait of Hormuz.
[...]
But even before his latest threat some countries had secured deals with Tehran for their vessels to use the key shipping route.
[...]
The critical route has become a global flashpoint after Tehran retaliated to US and Israeli airstrikes by threatening to attack ships in the strait.
[...]
through which a
[...]
Last week, Trump said the US did not need the Gulf's oil. He has repeatedly urged countries reliant on the region's energy to send warships to the strait and to take the lead to ensure shipments can resume.
[...]
In recent weeks - several Asian countries including Pakistan, India and the Philippines - have made agreements with Tehran to let some ships pass through the strait safely. China has also acknowledged that their vessels have also used the channel.
[...]
flagged ships
[...]
, said Theresa Lazaro, the South East Asian country's foreign affairs secretary.
[...]
Pakistan announced on 28 March that Iran has agreed to let 20 of its ships pass through the Strait of Hormuz.
[...]
Iran has openly welcomed India-flagged ships crossing the strait.
[...]
The embassy was responding to another post by its offices in South Africa saying that "only Iran and Oman" will decide the future of the Strait of Hormuz.
[...]
China, the biggest buyer of Iranian oil, also confirmed last week that some of its ships had crossed the strait, although it did not mention Iran or give more details on the vessels.
[...]
transited the
[...]
It is uncertain under what conditions some ships have negotiated safe passage - and whether they paid to cross the strait.
[...]
Over the weekend, a Japanese vessel carrying liquefied natural gas passed through the Strait of Hormuz, shipping firm Mitsui OSK Lines told the BBC.
[...]
"The safety of the vessel
[...]
have been confirmed," said the company, without commenting on whether any
[...]
In March, Malaysia also said some of its tankers have been cleared by Tehran to pass through the strait, with Prime Minister Anwar Ibrahim thanking Iran's president for facilitating the passage of its vessels.
[...]
The implications of these deals for other countries are still uncertain. For instance, whether other countries would switch their flags to those of countries being allowed to pass though.
[...]
Many tankers currently carry the flags of countries such as Panama and the Marshall Islands, which have not secured assurances of safe passage, Maniatis said.
[...]
It is still unknown just how lasting these assurances will be and how military operations in the region will impact them, he said.
results.summary:
Summary:
The Strait of Hormuz has become a focal point for maritime security as Tehran leverages guarantees of safe passage to influence global energy logistics. Following threats to disrupt shipping, several Asian nations have secured formal or informal arrangements with Iran to allow their vessels to transit the strait, including Pakistan, India, the Philippines, and to some extent China. The deals aim to ensure “safe, unhindered and expeditious passage” for their ships, recognizing their heavy reliance on Middle East energy. Uncertainty remains over the scope and durability of these assurances—whether they cover all ships or only specific flags—and whether Iran is distinguishing between a country’s political alliance and its participation in ongoing hostilities. The moves underscore the strategic importance of the corridor for oil and gas shipments (about a fifth of global energy trade) and signal a diplomatic approach to risk management in a volatile security environment. For watchstanding, key implications include: heightened need to monitor Iranian statements and potential escalations, assess which flag states have secured access and under what conditions, and evaluate ripple effects on tanker routing, insurance, and fleet readiness as regional tensions influence risk premiums and contingency planning.

results.title: Six MSC Vessels Slip Through Hormuz With AIS Dark Signals as Security Risks Escalate
results.author: Peter | Newsdesk
results.text:
Six MSC Vessels Slip Through Hormuz With AIS Dark Signals as Security Risks Escalate

[Industry Updates](https://breakbulk.news/category/weekly-updates/) [News Feed](https://breakbulk.news/category/news/)

# Six MSC Vessels Slip Through Hormuz With AIS Dark Signals as Security Risks Escalate

#### ByPeter | Newsdesk

Estimated reading time: 4 minutes

A rare and tightly controlled movement of container ships has drawn fresh attention to the security situation in the Strait of Hormuz, after six vessels operated by Mediterranean Shipping Company successfully exited the Gulf while transmitting no AIS signals during part of their journey.

According to shipping data cited by market analysts this week, the ships were part of a small group of eight container vessels that managed to transit the strait over the weekend. The operation took place during a narrow window when maritime traffic resumed under strict coordination following weeks of disruption linked to escalating regional tensions.

### Silent transit raises security questions

Automatic Identification System signals are a standard safety feature designed to broadcast a vessel’s position and identity. Turning them off is highly un
highlights:
A rare and tightly controlled movement of container ships has drawn fresh attention to the security situation in the Strait of Hormuz, after six vessels operated by Mediterranean Shipping Company successfully exited the Gulf while transmitting no AIS signals during part of their journey.
[...]
According to shipping data cited by market analysts this week, the ships were part of a small group of eight container vessels that managed to transit the strait over the weekend. The operation took place during a narrow window when maritime traffic resumed under strict coordination following weeks of disruption linked to escalating regional tensions.
[...]
Automatic Identification System signals are a standard safety feature designed to broadcast a vessel’s position and identity. Turning them off is highly unusual under normal commercial conditions. Yet in conflict sensitive areas, operators sometimes choose to go dark to reduce visibility and limit exposure to potential threats.
[...]
In this case, AIS transmissions from several vessels including the MSC operated ships were reportedly suspended during the most critical segment of the passage. Signals resumed only after the vessels had cleared the highest risk zone.
[...]
The transit occurred during a brief reopening of the Strait of Hormuz between April seventeen and April nineteen, following a period of severe disruption. Hundreds of vessels had been delayed or forced to hold position due to security concerns tied to tensions involving Iran and western aligned forces.
[...]
Shipping companies were given limited guidance on safe passage routes, often requiring coordination with naval authorities. Some operators chose to move quickly during the window, while others delayed voyages amid uncertainty over the stability of the situation.
[...]
all operators are
[...]
. Several global carriers have
[...]
avoid the Strait of Hormuz
[...]
Despite the successful transit of the eight container ships, overall traffic through the strait remains far below normal levels. Vessel tracking data shows only a gradual increase in movements, with many ships still waiting for clearer guidance.
[...]
Naval presence in the region remains high, and commercial shipping is effectively operating under monitored conditions. Reports indicate that vessels are being advised to follow designated corridors and maintain communication with authorities throughout transit.
[...]
The use of AIS shutdowns suggests that operators are adapting to a new risk environment where traditional safety practices may be temporarily altered in response to evolving threats.
results.summary:
Summary:
- Six MSC container ships briefly moved through the Strait of Hormuz with AIS dark signals, highlighting heightened security concerns in a previously congested transit window.
- AIS silence is rare and deliberate here to reduce visibility in a conflict-sensitive area; signals were re-enabled after clearing the high-risk zone.
- The incident underscores a strategic trade-off for operators: continued schedules and shorter transit times versus increased exposure to tracking, targeting, and security risk.
- Market reaction includes elevated war-risk insurance costs, potential rerouting around the Cape of Good Hope, and broader guidance from carriers to either avoid Hormuz or proceed with heightened caution.
- Overall, the Hormuz transit window (April 17–19) saw limited yet notable activity, with navies and authorities coordinating designated corridors and ongoing monitoring, reflecting a more risk-tolerant approach among some operators amid ongoing regional tensions.

results.title: Hormuz traffic rises to highest in weeks as more transits agreed | Financial Post
results.text:
Hormuz traffic rises to highest in weeks as more transits agreed | Financial Post

[Subscribe $20 for 1 year](https://financialpost.com/subscribe/recommended/subscribe-button/?utm_source=on-net&utm_medium=subscribe-button&utm_campaign=spring-sale&utm_content=)

A cargo ship carrying vehicles sails through the Arabian Gulf toward the Strait of Hormuz in the United Arab Emirates, Sunday, March 22, 2026. Photo by The Associated Press

Traffic through the [Strait of Hormuz](https://financialpost.com/tag/strait-of-hormuz/) has climbed to its highest levels since the early days of the war, as more countries secure apparent safe-passage agreements with Iran.

## Sign In or Create an Account

Email Address

Continue

or

The waterway saw 21 ships transit over the weekend as more energy-starved governments negotiate to extract vessels, cargoes and crews from the Persian Gulf, strengthening Tehran’s grip on the waterway. That’s the highest two-day total since the first days of March, when traffic was winding down.

We apologize, but this video has failed to load.

Try refreshing your browser, or [tap here to see other videos from our team](https://business.financialpost.com/video-centre/).
highlights:
Traffic through the Strait of Hormuz has climbed to its highest levels since the early days of the war, as more countries secure apparent safe-passage agreements with Iran.
[...]
The waterway saw 21 ships transit over the weekend as more energy-starved governments negotiate to extract vessels, cargoes and crews from the Persian Gulf, strengthening Tehran’s grip on the waterway. That’s the highest two-day total since the first days of March, when traffic was winding down.
[...]
Iranian vessels continue to dominate traffic, but on Sunday a tanker carrying Iraqi crude made its way through the strait, after Iran said it would grant an exemption to “brotherly Iraq.” India — which has negotiated the exit of some ships, and even taken Iranian liquefied petroleum gas (LPG) for the first time in years — has now seen eight of its LPG tankers come through.
[...]
Though the number of vessels is still a fraction of what it was before the war, when about 135 vessels were passing regularly each day, more countries are securing transits. Last week, two China-linked container ships made the crossing on a second attempt. Two Japan-linked vessels have also passed.
[...]
The Strait of Hormuz, a narrow corridor connecting the Persian Gulf with the rest of the world, has become a focal point as the war enters its sixth week, with United States President Donald Trump threatening to hit civilian infrastructure and bring “Hell” to Iran if it does not reopen the passage. Tehran has said it will only do so after the tolls that it’s charging vessels for transit can cover war damages.
[...]
Iran is also advancing a law governing its control of the strait and fees for passage, a move that formalizes an idiosyncratic payment system that has been in place for weeks, according to shipowners.
[...]
While Tehran is negotiating with friendly nations, the terms of these deals remain opaque. This is true even when the agreements are publicly recognized, as with Iraq over the weekend. It is even more so in cases where it is unclear which counterparties secured safe-passage, as with ships linked to France and Japan.
[...]
behind the Strait of Hormuz.
[...]
has been considering options including taking on other tankers and potentially re-flagging them to secure fertilizer, oil and other supplies.
[...]
To date, the majority of green-lit ships have taken what appears to be a route indicated by Tehran, hewing close to Iran’s coast. More, however, have begun taking a path along the opposite coastline. Oman, which shares the waters of the strait, confirmed on Sunday that it has held talks to smooth the flow.
results.summary:
Summary:
- The Strait of Hormuz is seeing rising shipping traffic for the first time in weeks, as more nations secure apparent safe-passage agreements with Iran. A weekend tally of 21 transits, including 13 outbound to the Arabian Sea, signals increasing activity though volumes remain well below pre-war levels.
- Iran continues to dominate transit control, leveraging tolls and formalizing passage rules; a newly advancing law would formalize its control and fees, reinforcing Tehran’s leverage over the waterway.
- Notable developments include: Iraq receiving an exemption, India facilitating several LPG and other tankers through Hormuz, and rare transits by China-linked and Japan-linked vessels on recent attempts.
- The dynamic remains volatile: US warnings of potential strikes against Iran’s civilian infrastructure persist, and Tehran has signaled it will reopen the strait only if transit tolls cover war damages. Underlying risk factors include possible escalation, opaque deal terms, and uncertain which carriers have secured safe-passage.
- For watchstanding: heightened attention should be given to (1) toll/fee policy developments and their enforcement, (2) which states and carriers secure transparent vs. opaque exemptions, and (3) potential flashpoints that could disrupt safe-passage or trigger rapid traffic shifts in the Hormuz corridor.
